events.title: Pedro de Valdivia Founds Santiago
events.detail: Spanish conquistador Pedro de Valdivia establishes the city of Santiago de Nueva Extremadura on February 12, 1541, on the banks of the Mapocho River near the Mapuche village of Quillicura. The founding marked the beginning of permanent Spanish colonization in central Chile, though the nascent colony faced immediate Mapuche resistance. Valdivia's settlement would become the nucleus of what eventually became the capital of an independent republic.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Arauco War — Mapuche Resistance Against Spanish Conquest
events.detail: The Arauco War — one of the longest armed conflicts in the Americas — intensifies as Mapuche confederations resist Spanish encroachment into Araucanía south of the Biobío River. The Mapuche, unlike most Andean and Mesoamerican peoples, successfully adopted Spanish military tactics including use of horses and captured steel weapons to wage an effective guerrilla campaign that would endure nearly 300 years in various forms.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Battle of Curalaba — Governor Óñez de Loyola Killed
events.detail: Mapuche forces led by toqui Pelantaro annihilate a Spanish column at Curalaba on December 23, 1598, killing Governor Martín García Óñez de Loyola and most of his forces. The victory triggered the 'Destruction of the Seven Cities,' pushing Spanish settlement north of the Biobío River and effectively creating a de facto Mapuche state south of that boundary for the following century.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Real Audiencia de Chile Established
events.detail: The Spanish Crown establishes a Real Audiencia (Royal Court of Justice) in Santiago, part of the Captaincy General of Chile which operated under the Viceroyalty of Peru. Chile's remote position at the edge of the Spanish empire, its difficult terrain, fierce Mapuche resistance, and lack of easily extractable mineral wealth (compared to Peru and Mexico) gave the colony a distinct character and fostered a self-reliant criollo identity.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Treaty of Quillín — Spain Recognizes Mapuche Autonomy
events.detail: Governor Francisco López de Zúñiga signs the Treaty of Quillín with Mapuche toquis — the first formal diplomatic agreement between the Spanish Crown and an indigenous nation in South America. Spain recognized Mapuche sovereignty south of the Biobío River and agreed to conduct annual 'parlamentos' (parliaments) rather than pursue endless military campaigns. The parlamento system became a unique form of colonial diplomacy lasting until independence.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Primera Junta de Gobierno — September 18, 1810
events.detail: Following Napoleon's invasion of Spain and the political vacuum created by the captivity of King Ferdinand VII, a cabildo abierto (open town council) in Santiago deposes the Spanish governor and establishes Chile's first national governing junta on September 18, 1810. While nominally still loyal to the king, this date is celebrated as Chilean Independence Day. The junta's creation launched the 'Patria Vieja' period of early self-governance.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Battle of Rancagua — Spanish Reconquista Begins
events.detail: Spanish royalist forces under General Mariano Osorio defeat Chilean patriot forces led by Bernardo O'Higgins and José Miguel Carrera at the Battle of Rancagua (October 1–2, 1814). The defeat ended the 'Patria Vieja' and began the 'Reconquista' — a brutal three-year period of Spanish repression during which many patriots fled to Argentina. The failure exposed deep divisions between the Carrera and O'Higgins factions that would haunt Chilean politics for decades.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Battle of Chacabuco — Liberation of Chile
events.detail: General José de San Martín leads the Army of the Andes — a force of Argentine and Chilean patriots — across the Andes in a feat of military logistics that surprised Spanish forces. On February 12, 1817, the patriots decisively defeat royalists at the Battle of Chacabuco, opening the road to Santiago. San Martín offers the governorship to O'Higgins, who accepts and is proclaimed Supreme Director. The victory effectively ends Spanish control of central Chile.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Formal Declaration of Chilean Independence
events.detail: Bernardo O'Higgins formally declares Chilean independence on February 12, 1818, the first anniversary of the Battle of Chacabuco. The declaration was ratified by plebiscite in April 1818. Following the Spanish defeat at the Battle of Maipú (April 5, 1818), independence was militarily secured. O'Higgins' subsequent authoritarian rule alienated the landowning elite and Church, leading to his abdication in 1823.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Portales and the Conservative Constitution of 1833
events.detail: Diego Portales, though rarely holding formal office, architects the Constitution of 1833 establishing a strong presidential republic with deep ties to the Catholic Church. The Portalian order — characterized by stability and elite rule — prevails for decades and makes Chile a relative exception to the endemic instability of other Latin American republics. Portales himself is assassinated in 1837, but his constitutional legacy endures until 1925.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: War of the Pacific — Chile vs. Bolivia and Peru
events.detail: Chile declares war on Bolivia (February 14, 1879) after Bolivia raises taxes on Chilean nitrate companies in the Atacama. Peru, bound by a secret alliance with Bolivia, enters the conflict. Chile's superior navy and well-trained army decisively defeat both adversaries. Bolivia loses its coastal access permanently — a dispute that festers to this day. Chile gains control of the nitrate-rich Atacama, launching an economic boom.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Battle of Iquique — Arturo Prat's Last Stand
events.detail: Chilean navy captain Arturo Prat refuses to surrender the aging wooden corvette Esmeralda to the ironclad Peruvian warship Huáscar on May 21, 1879. Prat boards the enemy ship during battle and is killed, becoming Chile's greatest naval hero. Though a tactical defeat, the Battle of Iquique became a defining moment of national identity, and May 21 is celebrated as Navy Day. Prat's sacrifice galvanized Chilean public opinion behind the war effort.

events.title: Treaty of Ancón — Chile Secures Atacama Territories
events.detail: The War of the Pacific ends with the Treaty of Ancón (1883) with Peru and Truce of Ancón with Bolivia (1884). Chile gains permanent sovereignty over Tarapacá from Peru and control of Antofagasta from Bolivia, leaving Bolivia landlocked — a grievance Bolivian governments raise internationally to this day. Chile's acquisition of Atacama's vast nitrate and copper deposits transforms it into a major South American power.

sources.name: Ministerio de Relaciones Exteriores de Chile

events.title: Pacificación de la Araucanía — Mapuche Military Defeat
events.detail: The Chilean military's 'Pacification of Araucanía' campaign (1861–1883) culminates in definitive defeat of Mapuche resistance by 1881–1883. Using modern rifles and coordinated military campaigns, Chile's army establishes forts and settler communities on Mapuche ancestral lands. The Mapuche are confined to 'reducciones' (reservations), losing an estimated 90% of their territory. Land hunger and dispossession fuel Mapuche grievances that persist to the present day.

events.title: Civil War of 1891 — End of Presidential Republic
events.detail: President José Manuel Balmaceda clashes with the nitrate oligarchy-dominated Congress over control of nitrate revenues and executive authority. Congress, backed by the navy, rebels in January 1891. After eight months of civil war, the congressional forces defeat Balmaceda's army. Balmaceda takes asylum in the Argentine embassy and commits suicide on September 19, 1891. Chile enters the 'Parliamentary Republic' era of oligarchic congressional dominance.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Iquique Massacre — Escuela Santa María de Iquique
events.detail: On December 21, 1907, Chilean army troops massacre striking nitrate workers and their families gathered at the Escuela Santa María de Iquique. Workers had descended from the pampa salitreras demanding an end to token (ficha) wages and improved safety conditions. Estimates of the dead range from several hundred to 3,600. The massacre became a foundational trauma for the Chilean labor movement and left-wing politics.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Military Intervention and New Constitution (1924–1925)
events.detail: A 'ruido de sables' (rattling of sabers) by junior military officers in 1924 forces Congress to pass long-delayed labor legislation. In 1925, a new constitution replaces the Portalian 1833 text — restoring presidential power, separating Church and State, and establishing social rights. The period marks Chile's transition from oligarchic parliamentarism toward a more interventionist state.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Great Depression Devastates Chilean Nitrate Economy
events.detail: The Great Depression (1929–1933) collapses global nitrate demand, shattering the economy that funded Chilean public finances for 50 years. Chile is among the hardest-hit countries globally — exports fall by 80%, unemployment soars, and the peso collapses. Chile has five presidents in 1931–1932. The crisis accelerates the transition to copper as Chile's primary export, as the nitrate industry never recovers its pre-WWI dominance.

events.title: Popular Front Victory — Pedro Aguirre Cerda Elected
events.detail: The Frente Popular — a coalition of Radical, Socialist, and Communist parties — wins the 1938 presidential election with Pedro Aguirre Cerda by a margin of 4,111 votes. The victory breaks 16 years of Conservative-Liberal rule and launches an era of state-led industrialization (ISI model) through CORFO (Chilean Development Corporation, founded 1939). The Popular Front period establishes Chile's mixed economy model and expands the middle class.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Frei Montalva and Christian Democracy's 'Revolution in Liberty'
events.detail: Eduardo Frei Montalva's Christian Democratic Party wins 56% of the vote in 1964 in a bipolar election designed to block Allende. His 'Revolution in Liberty' advances land reform (Ley de Reforma Agraria 1967), 'Chileanization' of copper (51% state ownership of Anaconda and Kennecott mines), and massive expansion of public education. The PDC's reforms galvanize both left (calling them insufficient) and right (calling them dangerous), polarizing Chilean politics.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Salvador Allende Wins Presidency — World's First Elected Marxist
events.detail: Salvador Allende wins the September 4, 1970 election by a plurality of 36.3% with the Unidad Popular coalition, defeating conservative Jorge Alessandri (34.9%) and Christian Democrat Radomiro Tomic (27.8%). The CIA, under Nixon's order ('Make the economy scream'), backs a failed coup attempt (Track II) to prevent Allende's ratification. General René Schneider is assassinated. Congress ratifies Allende with a 'Statute of Constitutional Guarantees.'

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Copper Nationalization — 'El Sueldo de Chile'
events.detail: Chile's Congress unanimously passes Allende's nationalization of the large copper mines (La Gran Minería del Cobre) on July 11, 1971 — thereafter celebrated as 'Día del Cobre.' The state copper company CODELCO takes over the Anaconda and Kennecott properties at Chuquicamata, El Teniente, and El Salvador. The nationalization is the most popular measure of Allende's government but triggers Nixon's economic pressure campaign.

events.title: Economic Crisis and Opposition Escalation
events.detail: By 1972, Chile's economy is under severe strain: the US copper blockade and credit freeze; accelerating land expropriation disrupting agricultural supply; and a CIA/ITT-backed truckers' strike (October 1972) that paralyzed the economy for 26 days. Annual inflation reaches 163% by year end. The Christian Democrats and Nationals form a Democratic Confederation to block Allende in Congress. The polarization deepens as the military begins planning a coup.

events.title: General Schneider Assassinated in CIA-Backed Plot
events.detail: Army Commander-in-Chief General René Schneider is shot on October 22, 1970, by a CIA-backed group attempting to kidnap him to provoke a coup and prevent Allende's congressional ratification. Schneider dies three days later. The plot — coordinated between CIA operatives and Chilean extreme-right groups — backfired: Schneider's death galvanized constitutionalist sentiment, and Congress ratified Allende. Declassified documents confirm CIA payment to perpetrators.

events.title: Military Coup — September 11, 1973
events.detail: Chilean armed forces and Carabineros launch a coup d'état on the morning of September 11, 1973. Air force Hawker Hunter jets bomb La Moneda presidential palace. Allende dies inside — forensic examiners later conclude by suicide with an AK-47 gifted by Fidel Castro. The junta — led by Generals Pinochet and Leigh, Admiral Merino, and Carabineros Director Mendoza — suspends Congress, bans political parties, and declares a state of siege. An estimated 30,000 people are arrested in the first weeks.

events.title: Estadio Nacional — Mass Detention Center Post-Coup
events.detail: In the weeks following the coup, Chile's national football stadium (Estadio Nacional) is converted into a detention and torture center holding 7,000+ political prisoners. Folk singer Víctor Jara is tortured and executed on September 16, 1973. The Estadio Chile (renamed Estadio Víctor Jara) held an estimated 5,000 additional prisoners. The systematic use of sports facilities as torture centers was documented by the Rettig Commission.

events.title: Caravan of Death — Extrajudicial Executions
events.detail: In October 1973, a military helicopter-borne death squad tours northern Chilean cities under Pinochet's close aide General Sergio Arellano Stark. The caravan summarily executes 97 political prisoners who had already been sentenced by local military courts, replacing prison terms with death sentences. Bodies were buried in unmarked graves or thrown into the sea. The Caravan of Death established early that Pinochet personally directed extrajudicial killings.

events.title: DINA Created — Operation Condor Transnational Repression Network
events.detail: The Dirección de Inteligencia Nacional (DINA) is formally established by Decree Law No. 521 in June 1974 under Colonel Manuel Contreras, reporting directly to Pinochet. DINA operates Villa Grimaldi and a network of secret detention sites. In 1975, Pinochet co-founds Operation Condor with Argentina, Uruguay, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Brazil — a transnational network for tracking and eliminating leftist dissidents across borders. Former Chilean ambassador Orlando Letelier is assassinated by DINA agents in Washington D.C. in 1976.

events.title: Chicago Boys — Neoliberal Shock Therapy
events.detail: From 1975 onward, Chilean economists trained at the University of Chicago under Milton Friedman (the 'Chicago Boys,' led by Sergio de Castro) implement radical free-market reforms: trade liberalization, privatization of state enterprises, financial deregulation, reduction of tariffs from 94% to 10%, and pension privatization (AFPs, 1980). Chile becomes a global laboratory for neoliberal economics. The 1975 'shock treatment' caused GDP to fall 13% and the 1982 debt crisis required state bailouts — after which some reforms were adjusted.

events.title: Pinochet's 1980 Constitution — Locked-In Neoliberal Order
events.detail: A disputed referendum on September 11, 1980 (the coup's seventh anniversary, with no electoral registry or opposition campaigning) approves a new constitution. The 1980 Constitution enshrines private property and the subsidiary state; creates unelected senators; requires 2/3 supermajorities to amend most articles (the 'llave de candado'); extends Pinochet's rule; and enshrines the AFP pension privatization. Critics argue the constitution was designed to make neoliberal reforms irreversible.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: 1988 Plebiscite — 'NO' Defeats Pinochet
events.detail: On October 5, 1988, Chileans vote in the plebiscite mandated by the 1980 constitution on whether Pinochet should continue for another 8-year term. The 'NO' campaign, using innovative TV commercials emphasizing joy and hope ('Chile, la alegría ya viene'), wins 55.99% vs 44.01% for 'SÍ.' A dictator leaves power through a process he himself designed — a pivotal moment in democratic history. Pinochet initially resists accepting the result but ultimately concedes.

events.title: Rettig Commission — Truth and Reconciliation Report
events.detail: President Patricio Aylwin establishes the National Truth and Reconciliation Commission under Raúl Rettig. The 1991 Rettig Report documents 3,197 cases of human rights violations resulting in death or disappearance between 1973 and 1990. Aylwin's nationally televised address acknowledging the crimes of the state and asking forgiveness from victims' families became a landmark moment in transitional justice.

sources.name: Comisión Nacional de Verdad y Reconciliación (Rettig)

events.title: Pinochet Arrested in London — Extradition Battle
events.detail: On October 16, 1998, Pinochet is arrested in London at the request of Spanish judge Baltasar Garzón who seeks his extradition for crimes against Spanish citizens. The House of Lords rules that a former head of state has no immunity for torture. After 16 months, UK Home Secretary Jack Straw releases Pinochet on humanitarian grounds (January 2000). Pinochet returns to Chile where he subsequently loses immunity in over 300 criminal cases before dying in 2006.

events.title: Valech Commission — 40,000 Torture Victims Documented
events.detail: President Ricardo Lagos establishes the National Commission on Political Imprisonment and Torture under Bishop Sergio Valech in 2003. The first Valech Report (2004) recognizes 27,255 victims of political imprisonment and torture; a second report (2011) adds 9,795 more, reaching 40,280 recognized victims. Perpetrators are named but their identities are sealed for 50 years. The commission reveals the systematic nature of sexual violence as a political weapon.

sources.name: Comisión Nacional sobre Prisión Política y Tortura (Valech)

events.title: Lagos Reforms 1980 Constitution — Removes Pinochet-Era Provisions
events.detail: President Ricardo Lagos signs comprehensive amendments to the 1980 Constitution in September 2005, removing the most controversial provisions: eliminating designated senators, restricting presidential power over military commanders, abolishing the National Security Council's political veto, and reasserting civilian supremacy. Lagos symbolically signs the new text with his name rather than Pinochet's, declaring 'Chile now has a democratic constitution.'

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Estallido Social — October 2019 Uprising
events.detail: What begins as a student fare-dodging protest against a 30-peso metro fare increase on October 18, 2019 erupts into Chile's worst social crisis since the dictatorship. Millions march in the streets, a state of emergency is declared. President Piñera says 'we are at war with a powerful enemy.' The deep causes are structural: Chile's extreme inequality (Gini 0.47), unaffordable pensions, health costs, and a sense that democratic transition benefits primarily accrued to the elite. As the slogan went: 'No son 30 pesos, son 30 años.'

events.title: Police Repression of 2019 Protests — Systematic Eye Injuries
events.detail: The IACHR documents systematic human rights violations during the 2019 uprising: 29 deaths (6 directly attributed to security forces), 2,521 eye injuries (many causing permanent blindness from rubber bullet shots to the face), 8,827 total injured, and 2,840 arrests in the first weeks. HRW and Amnesty International condemn Carabineros' systematic use of anti-riot ammunition against protestors' heads. President Piñera eventually fires Interior Minister Andrés Chadwick and General Director Mario Rozas.

events.title: Agreement for Social Peace and New Constitution
events.detail: On November 15, 2019, after weeks of unrest, Chilean political parties sign the 'Agreement for Social Peace and a New Constitution' in Congress. The accord calls for a referendum on replacing the 1980 Pinochet constitution and whether a convention should write it. The agreement channels the uprising's energy toward institutional reform, defusing the immediate crisis but creating a complex multi-year constitutional process.

sources.name: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile

events.title: Gabriel Boric Wins Presidential Election
events.detail: Gabriel Boric, 35, a former student protest leader and Frente Amplio congressman, wins the December 19, 2021 runoff election against far-right José Antonio Kast (52.2% vs 47.8%) — the largest turnout in Chilean history (55%). Boric is the youngest president in Chilean history. His Apruebo Dignidad coalition promises pension reform, expanded social rights, environmental protections, and a new constitution. Inaugurated March 11, 2022.

events.title: Draft Constitution Rejected — 62% Vote 'Rechazo'
events.detail: On September 4, 2022, Chileans vote on the new constitutional draft produced by the elected Constitutional Convention (May 2021–July 2022). The draft — including rights to water, housing, health, parity governance, and plurinational recognition of indigenous peoples — is rejected by 61.9% ('Rechazo') vs 38.1% ('Apruebo'). The rejection is attributed to controversial provisions, media campaigns, and 'draft fatigue' among voters who found the 388-article text too radical.

events.title: Second Constitutional Draft Also Rejected — 55.8% 'En Contra'
events.detail: After the 2022 rejection, Chile launches a second constitutional process. A new Constitutional Council dominated by the far-right Republican Party produces a considerably more conservative draft. It is also rejected in the December 17, 2023 plebiscite with 55.8% voting 'En Contra' vs 44.2% 'A Favor.' Chile's constitution-making process ends in failure from both left and right, leaving the country governed by the amended 1980 constitution.

events.title: Mapuche-Araucanía Conflict — State of Emergency Renewed
events.detail: The Araucanía and Bío Bío regions experience escalating violence as Mapuche radical groups (associated with the Coordinadora Arauco Malleco / CAM) burn forestry equipment, attack police, and destroy farmland to demand return of ancestral territories. Both Piñera (2019, 2021) and Boric (2022–) repeatedly renew states of emergency. Human rights organizations criticize both Mapuche violence and state impunity. Boric's promised 'political solution' remains elusive as the conflict continues in 2026.

events.title: Boric's National Lithium Strategy — State Partnership Model
events.detail: In April 2023, President Boric announces Chile's national lithium strategy: ENAMI (state mining company) will hold majority equity in new lithium contracts, while existing SQM and Albemarle contracts continue until expiry (2030 and 2043 respectively). The strategy aims to capture more value from Chile's 36% share of world lithium reserves without full nationalization. The policy responds to the global electric vehicle battery demand surge but faces criticism from both sides — too much state for industry, too little for the left.
